_comment: Template/example for high-level discovery artifact JSON. Used by the requirement-discovery agent. Template path: requirements/requirement-discovery/templates/requirement-discovery.schema.json. Save to: requirements/discovery/YYYY-MM-DD-{slug}.discovery.json
title: Lab Booking System

dimensions.name: Stakeholders
dimensions.notes: Some governance roles still need confirmation.

dimensions.name: Scope
dimensions.notes: Most boundaries agreed; one external integration is still unclear.

dimensions.name: Current State
dimensions.notes: Pain points and current process are well understood at a high level.

dimensions.name: Future State Intent
dimensions.notes: Core capabilities known, reporting expectations still broad.

dimensions.name: Business Rules / Constraints
dimensions.notes: Policy constraints confirmed; some timing rules are assumed.

dimensions.name: Assumptions / Open Questions
dimensions.notes: Several questions remain before downstream decomposition.

problem_statement: Academic staff and students cannot easily discover which laboratory equipment is available or book it in advance, leading to scheduling conflicts and underutilisation of shared resources.
business_outcome: Provide a business-friendly self-service booking capability that improves visibility of equipment availability, reduces scheduling conflicts, and supports better use of shared resources.

stakeholders.involvement: Books equipment for coursework or research.
Books equipment and may participate in approval decisions for restricted usage.
Oversees equipment availability rules and operational readiness.
[Assumed] Supports enabling systems and institutional service dependencies.

in_scope: Viewing equipment availability by date and time
Supporting approval handling for restricted equipment
Providing notifications for important booking events
out_of_scope: Procurement or inventory management of equipment

current_state_summary: Equipment availability is not consistently visible to staff and students before they make a request.
Booking coordination relies on manual communication and local knowledge held by operational teams.
pain_points: Users experience scheduling conflicts because availability is not transparent.
Operational staff spend time manually coordinating and resolving clashes.
Restricted equipment requests are not handled through a consistent business process.
future_state_intent: Users should be able to view availability and submit booking requests through a clear self-service process.
Restricted equipment should follow a defined approval path with better visibility.
Stakeholders should receive clearer notifications and higher-level oversight of booking activity.

business_rules_constraints.statement: A booking must not overlap with an existing confirmed booking for the same equipment.
Restricted equipment requests must be reviewed before they are confirmed.
[Assumed] Requests should normally be submitted at least 24 hours before the requested booking start time.

dependencies.description: Operational rules and availability information must come from Lab Administration.
[Assumed] Institutional authentication and email services will be reused where possible.

assumptions.statement: IT Services will support the business capability using existing institutional services where feasible.
Single sign-on can be reused for the future process.
The institution's email service can support booking notifications.

open_questions.question: Should bookings be cancellable after the start time?
Is there a maximum booking duration per equipment type?
Should recurring bookings be part of the initial business scope?
